content.text: Processes essential for the survival of living beings—like nutrition, respiration, excretion, and reproduction—are collectively called life processes. Animals, including humans, obtain energy from food, which allows them to carry out these processes.

Animals eat different types of food: bees and sunbirds suck nectar, infants feed on their mother's milk, pythons swallow their prey whole, and some aquatic animals filter tiny floating food particles.

The food consumed contains complex components (like carbohydrates, proteins, and fats) which must be broken down into simpler forms for the body to use. This breaking down occurs in a long tube called the alimentary canal, which starts at the mouth and ends at the anus. As food moves through this canal, digestive juices secrete and break it down, allowing it to be absorbed and transported.

topics.topic_name: 9.1.1 Digestion in human beings - The Mouth Cavity

content.text: The journey of food begins in the mouth. Teeth break the food into smaller pieces by crushing and chewing—this initial breakdown is called mechanical digestion.

content.title: Let us investigate — Action of saliva on starch
procedure: Place a teaspoonful of boiled rice in A.
Chew a teaspoonful of boiled rice for 30-60 seconds and place it in B.
Add 3-4 mL of water to both and record initial colour.
Add 3-4 drops of iodine solution to each tube, mix, and observe.
content.observation: Test tube A (boiled rice) turns blue-black. Test tube B (chewed rice) either does not change colour or turns a very light blue-black.
content.conclusion: The blue-black colour in A indicates the presence of starch. In B, chewing mixed the rice with saliva, which breaks down complex starch into simple sugars, removing the starch and preventing the intense colour change. This chemical breakdown of complex components into simpler forms in the body is called digestion.

content.title: Science and Society — Oral Hygiene
content.text: A healthy mouth requires brushing teeth and cleaning the tongue twice a day, and rinsing the mouth with water after meals to prevent tooth decay and bad smell.

Saliva moistens chewed food, making it easy to swallow. The tongue pushes it into the food pipe (oesophagus). The walls of the food pipe gently contract and relax in a wave-like motion to push the food forward into the stomach.

In the stomach, the walls contract and relax to churn the food. It mixes with mucosal secretions containing digestive juice, acid, and mucus. The juice breaks down proteins into simpler components. The acid further helps digestion and kills harmful bacteria, while mucus protects the stomach lining from its own acid. Here, food becomes a semi-liquid mass.

content.title: Fascinating Facts — Alexis St. Martin's Stomach
content.text: In 1822, Alexis St. Martin was accidentally shot. The wound never fully healed, leaving a small hole into his stomach. Dr. William Beaumont used this opening to directly observe human digestion, conducting experiments on how food breaks down and studying the effects of emotions on digestion.

The partially digested food enters the small intestine, the longest part of the alimentary canal (almost 6 metres long). It receives digestive secretions from its own inner lining, the liver, and the pancreas.

content.title: Secretions in the Small Intestine
items: Liver: Secretes mildly basic bile, which neutralises stomach acids and breaks down fats into tiny droplets.
Pancreas: Secretes basic pancreatic juice to neutralise acid and further break down carbohydrates, proteins, and fats.
Intestinal Wall: Secretes juices to finalize the breakdown of these nutrients into simpler forms.

content.text: The inner lining of the small intestine is thin and covered in thousands of finger-like projections that increase surface area. Digested nutrients are absorbed through these projections into blood vessels, which carry them to the rest of the body.

content.title: Science and Society — Celiac Disease
content.text: Celiac disease is a condition where the body reacts negatively to gluten (found in wheat, barley, and rye), severely damaging the nutrient-absorbing lining of the small intestine. Patients must stick to a gluten-free diet, using alternatives like millets (jowar, bajra, ragi).

What remains is undigested food, which then moves into the large intestine. At 1.5 metres long, it is shorter but wider than the small intestine. Its role is to absorb water and some salts, turning the waste into a semi-solid stool. This is stored in the rectum and eventually expelled through the anus in a process called egestion.

content.title: Fascinating Facts — Beneficial Gut Bacteria
content.text: The large intestine houses important bacteria that help break down remaining undigested food (especially fibre) and produce essential nutrients. Eating fermented foods (curd, buttermilk, pickles, kanji) and high-fibre diets aids these bacteria and promotes a healthy digestive system.

content.title: Science and Society — The Charaka Samhita
content.text: The ancient Ayurvedic text, Charaka Samhita, recognized the importance of digestion, advocating for easily digestible foods and spices like ginger, black pepper, and cumin. Modern science echoes this by emphasizing proper meal times, mindful eating, and avoiding overeating.

Grass-eating animals like cows and buffaloes are called ruminants. They quickly swallow partially chewed grass into their stomachs for initial digestion, then bring the partially digested food back into their mouths to chew it thoroughly later. This process is called rumination.

Birds lack teeth. Instead, they digest food using a muscular chamber called a gizzard. The gizzard walls contract and relax to break down food, often utilizing swallowed grit (small stones) to help grind it.

topics.topic_name: 9.2.1 Respiration in humans - How do we breathe?

content.text: Inhaling and exhaling air is called breathing. The human respiratory system creates a specific pathway: air enters through pairs of nasal openings called nostrils, moves through the nasal passages (where tiny hair and mucus trap dirt and dust), travels down the windpipe, and enters the two lungs. In the lungs, branches divide finally ending in balloon-like sacs called alveoli. The rib cage protects the lungs.

content.title: Let us make a model — Mechanism of breathing
procedure: Take a plastic bottle with its bottom cut off. Seal a Y-shaped tube into its cap.
Attach two deflated balloons to the branches of the Y-tube inside the bottle.
Cover the wide bottom of the bottle tightly with a rubber sheet.
Pull the rubber sheet downwards, then release it upwards, and observe the balloons.
content.observation: Pulling the sheet downwards inflates the balloons. Releasing it upwards deflates them.
content.conclusion: The balloons represent lungs and the rubber sheet represents the diaphragm. When inhaling, the chest expands (ribs move up/out, diaphragm moves down), increasing chest space and pulling air into the lungs. When exhaling, ribs move down/in and the diaphragm moves up, pushing air out.

content.title: Science and Society — Breathing Practices
content.text: Breathing exercises (like Pranayama) improve respiratory health and mind relaxation. In Ladakh, the Tummo breathing technique helps people stay warm in freezing weather. Deep breathing and chanting use rhythmic breath control for mental clarity.

topics.topic_name: What do we breathe out and How does gas exchange happen?

content.title: Let us explore — Contents of exhaled air
procedure: Place equal fresh lime water in test tubes A and B.
In A, pump surrounding air using a syringe/pichkari.
In B, forcefully blow exhaled air through a straw from your mouth.
content.observation: The lime water in tube B turns milky/cloudy, while tube A does not.
content.conclusion: Lime water turns milky when reacting with carbon dioxide. This proves that exhaled air contains a significantly higher amount of carbon dioxide than inhaled air.

content.text: Gas exchange occurs in the alveoli. The thin walls of alveoli are surrounded by blood vessels. Oxygen from inhaled air passes into the blood to be transported around the body, while carbon dioxide is released from the blood into the alveoli to be exhaled.

Breathing brings in oxygen, but respiration is the chemical process that effectively uses that oxygen to break down food (glucose) into usable energy, along with carbon dioxide and water. The word equation is:
Glucose + Oxygen → Carbon dioxide + Water + Energy

content.title: Science and Society — Smoking
content.text: Smoking damages the lungs, frequently causing lung cancer and other respiratory diseases. It also massively endangers non-smokers (including children and pregnant women) through passive smoking of toxic chemicals released into the air.

topics.topic_name: Circulatory System and 9.2.2 Respiration in other animals

content.text: The circulatory system (heart, blood, blood vessels) coordinates with the respiratory and digestive systems, ensuring that oxygen and digested nutrients are transported to all body parts while wastes are efficiently removed.

Different animals have vastly different breathing mechanisms. Mammals, birds, and reptiles typically use lungs. Most aquatic animals like fish use gills, which are highly vascularised structures that extract oxygen directly dissolved in water. Amphibians (like frogs) use gills as tadpoles, but develop lungs and can also exchange gases directly through their moist skin as adults. Earthworms also use their moist skin exclusively for breathing.

glossary.definition: The vital processes essential for the survival of living beings, collectively including nutrition, circulation, respiration, excretion, and reproduction.
The long continuous tube starting at the mouth and ending at the anus through which food passes and is digested in the body.
The process of breaking down complex food components into simpler forms so they can be absorbed and utilized by the body.
The physical process of breaking down food into fine pieces through crushing and chewing by the teeth.
The long, flexible food pipe that connects the mouth cavity to the stomach, transferring food via wave-like muscular contractions.
The digestive process seen in ruminants (like cows) where partially chewed and swallowed food is brought back up to the mouth from the stomach to be thoroughly chewed.
A muscular chamber in the digestive tract of birds that contracts and relaxes, frequently using swallowed stones, to break down and grind food.
The physical process of inhaling air (rich in oxygen) into the lungs and exhaling air (rich in carbon dioxide) out of the lungs.
The chemical process constantly occurring inside the body where oxygen is used to break down glucose (food) into carbon dioxide and water to release usable energy.
The tiny, thin-walled, balloon-like sacs situated at the end of the finest branches in the lungs, where the actual exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide gases happens with the blood.
A large, dome-shaped muscle located directly below the lungs that heavily assists the mechanics of breathing by moving upwards and downwards.
The bodily system comprising the heart, blood, and blood vessels responsible for powerfully transporting nutrients, oxygen, and wastes throughout the body.

summary_points: Life processes like nutrition, respiration, circulation, and excretion are vital for the continuous survival and functioning of all animals.
The human digestive system fundamentally consists of the vast alimentary canal (mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, anus) and its associated glands (liver, pancreas).

Digestion physically and chemically breaks down complex food components into simpler nutrients, which are primarily absorbed through the finger-like projections in the thin walls of the small intestine.
The large intestine mainly absorbs water and salts from undigested waste prior to egestion.
Herbivores like cows act as ruminants uniquely designed to re-chew partially digested food, while birds rely heavily on muscular gizzards since they lack teeth.
The human respiratory system primarily utilizes lungs, where oxygen is efficiently exchanged for waste carbon dioxide inside thousands of tiny alveolar sacs.
While breathing is the physical mechanical process of moving air in and out, respiration is the internal chemical process that effectively utilizes oxygen to extract robust energy from glucose.
Distinct animals possess highly adapted breathing mechanisms for their specific environments, such as gills for aquatic fish or moist skin for earthworms.
